rulings.Case Title: RANDALL WHITNEY VS. METROPOLITAN TRANSPORTATION COMMISSION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Thursday, May 23, 2019, line 1. DEFENDANT METROPOLITAN TRANSPORTATION COMMISSION'S MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS. Defendant's motion for judgment on the pleadings is ordered OFF CALENDAR to enable compliance with CCP §439, which requires a "meet and confer in person or by telephone." Based on the declaration of defendant's counsel, it does not appear that such a meet and confer occurred. The parties are ordered to meet and confer immediately. Defendant's response to the petition is due by June 24, 2019. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On Petition For Approval Of Transfer Of Structured Settlement Payment Rights
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Thursday, May 23, 2019, line 2. PETITIONER SEQUOIA COMPANIES, LLC'S Petition For Approval Of Transfer Of Structured Settlement Payment Rights. CONTINUED to July 12, 2019. Berkshire Hathaway Life Insurance Company of Nebraska states that its affiliated entity (BIFCO) is willing to give Pedro S. far more consideration for the assignment than Sequoia Companies, LLC. Therefore, it is presently not apparent whether the transfer of the structured settlement "is in the best interest of the payee." (Ins. Code § 10139.5(a)(1). The petition for approval is CONTINUED to allow Pedro S. to explore this other option. Petitioner and Berkshire have leave to file supplemental briefs by July 5, 2019. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: CHEONG KONG VS. DR. SAMUEL CHI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Dismiss Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law and Motion Calendar for Thursday, May 23, 2019, line 5. DEFENDANTS THIENKHAI VU, M.D., DOUGLAS HANKS, M.D.'S Motion To Dismiss Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint. Defendants Thienkhai Vu, M.D. and Douglas Hanks, M.D.'s CCP §583.210 motion to dismiss plaintiff Cheong Kong's first amended complaint is GRANTED. Kong's original complaint naming Doe defendants was filed September 29, 2015; he then had three years to identify and serve Drs. Vu and Hanks as Does to avoid mandatory dismissal. (Id. ("shall be served"); Inversiones Papaluchi S.A.S. v. Sup. Ct. (2018) 20 Cal.App.5th 1055, 1061.) Absent tolling, the date by which Kong had to serve Vu and Hanks was September 29, 2018, and he did not serve them until March 11, 2019. CCP §583.240 provides the grounds for tolling the service-of-process time limit. Kong says Vu and Hanks were "not amenable" to service and it was "impossible" to timely serve them "due to causes beyond his control." (See id.) But he cites no facts or law to support either point. Kong's counsel describes difficulty ascertaining the rules regarding Doe amendments, but he did master them eventually, albeit belatedly. The appellate courts require that these service statutes "be construed strictly against the plaintiff." (Shipley v. Sugita (1996) 50 Cal.App.4th 320, 326.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: JANET M. PLANELLS VS. STATE OF CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant City And County Of San Francisco'S Motion For Protective Order Regarding Deposition Of Jason Jackson And Category 8 Of Plaintiff'S Ccp 2025.230 Deposition Notice
rulings.Rulings: Defendant City And County Of San Francisco'S Motion For Protective Order Regarding Deposition Of Jason Jackson And Category 8 Of Plaintiff'S Ccp 2025.230 Deposition Notice Pro Tem Judge William Lynn, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Parties to appear. Moving party should be prepared to discuss why moving party's suppositions should be taken as facts supporting the motion. Opposing party should be prepared to discuss the issue of relevance of discovery sought. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to wlynn@rroyselaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (JPT/RBU/302).

rulings.Case Title: JOHN DG VS. DOE 1 ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion Of Confidentiality Protective Order
rulings.Rulings: INTERVENOR COE 1 Notice Of Motion Of Confidentiality Protective Order Pro Tem Judge William Lynn, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion denied. The motion is premature and the statutory scheme provides sufficient privacy safeguards to Intervener Coe 1. The parties should consider stipulating to a modified confidentiality protective order as indicated in the papers. The court will sign an order for stipulated confidentiality protective order. The parties need not appear on May 23 if the tentative ruling denying the motion is not contested or if the parties submit a stipulated confidentiality protective order by email to the Pro Tem Judge no later than 4pm on May 22, 2019. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to wlynn@rroyselaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (JPT/RBU/302)

rulings.Case Title: DWIGHT A. LUM ET AL VS. ZEPPELIN W. WONG ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Dwight A. Lums Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents, Set Four
rulings.Rulings: DEFENDANT VELDA WONG's Motion To Compel Dwight A. Lums Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents, Set Four Pro Tem Judge William Lynn, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion granted and sanctions awarded as requested. Moving party has made sufficient showing of relevance and good cause for production of documents while opposing party has not made sufficient showing of having acted with substantial justification. Responding party shall provide complete, verified responses, produce all responsive documents, and remit payment of sanctions to moving party within 15 days after notice of entry of this order. If this tentative ruling is contested, opposing party should be prepared to address the issues of: 1) whether it is a serious invasion of opposing party's privacy; and 2) whether opposing party acted with substantial justification. Opposing party should also be prepared to address Williams v. Superior Court (2017) 3 Cal. 5th 531. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to wlynn@rroyselaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (JPT/RBU/302)

rulings.Case Title: LEERINK REVELATION HEALTHCARE FUND I, L.P ET AL VS. BALAJI K SRINIVASAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Responses To Special Interrogatory No. 4 From Plaintiff Revelation Healthcare Fund I, L.P. And Plaintiff Revelation Healthcare Fund Ii, L.P.
rulings.Rulings: DEFENDANT BALAJI SRINIVASAN's Motion To Compel Responses To Special Interrogatory No. 4 From Plaintiff Revelation Healthcare Fund I, L.P. And Plaintiff Revelation Healthcare Fund Ii, L.P. Pro Tem Judge William Lynn, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion granted. Sanctions not requested and not awarded. Moving party's Special Interrogatory No. 4 covers a single subject matter and therefore does not violate the "rule of 35." Clement v. Alegre (2009) 177 Cal. App. 4th 1277. This case is not at all similar to the situation in Catanese v. Superior Court (1996) 46 Cal. App. 4th 1159. Opposing parties' objections on the grounds of overbroad, compound, and vagueness/ambiguous are without merit. Opposing party appears to have "deliberately misconstrued the question." Deyo v. Kilbourne (1978) 84 Cal. App. 3d 771, 783. Opposing party is ordered to provide complete, verified responses to Special Interrogatory No. 4 within 15 days after notice of entry of this order. Sanctions not requested and not awarded. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to wlynn@rroyselaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (JPT/RBU/302)
